home:
The Arcipelago Ceresa project aims to create a digital edition of the unpublished, dispersed, and posthumous writings of the Swiss Italian writer Alice Ceresa (1923-2001), while simultaneously developing new critical investigations into her work.

A figure of extraordinary originality in twentieth-century literature, Alice Ceresa distinguished herself through her multilingualism and cultural activity within a transnational horizon. An anticipator of central themes in queer and gender debates, she pursued relentlessly experimental formal research that places her among the most innovative voices of her generation. Her archive holds numerous unpublished texts, while the corpus of published writings remains largely inaccessible to contemporary readers.

The digital edition, created in close collaboration with the Data Science Lab (DSL) of the University of Bern and the Swiss Literary Archives (SLA), will make available to readers not only a rich selection of unpublished and dispersed writings, but also a new version of the posthumous texts, including radio readings from the period.

The editorial work will be accompanied by four research projects dedicated to aspects of Ceresa's production that remain underexplored, yet are of particular significance for contemporary scholarship. The projects will investigate, on the one hand, the author’s multilingualism and transnational dimension; on the other, the relationship established in her writing between the deconstruction of literary genres and that of gender roles.
even_stale_liger_read: Alice Ceresa in her studio, ca. 1966, ASL-Ceresa-C-3-c/1
project_description_text:
The Arcipelago Ceresa project aims to create a digital edition of the unpublished, dispersed, and posthumous writings of the Swiss Italian writer Alice Ceresa (1923-2001), while simultaneously developing new critical investigations into her work.

A figure of extraordinary originality in twentieth-century literature, Alice Ceresa distinguished herself through her multilingualism and cultural activity within a transnational horizon. An anticipator of central themes in queer and gender debates, she pursued a relentlessly experimental formal research that places her among the most innovative voices of her generation. Her archive holds numerous unpublished texts, while the corpus of published writings remains largely inaccessible to contemporary readers.

The digital edition, created in close collaboration with the Data Science Lab (DSL) of the University of Bern and the Swiss Literary Archives (SLA), will make available to readers not only a rich selection of unpublished and dispersed writings, but also a new version of the posthumous texts, including radio readings from the period.

The editorial work will be accompanied by four research projects dedicated to aspects of Ceresa's production that remain underexplored, yet are of particular significance for contemporary scholarship. The projects will investigate, on the one hand, the author’s multilingualism and transnational dimension; on the other, the relationship established in her writing between the deconstruction of literary genres and that of gender roles
dsl_text:
The digital edition project is being carried out in close collaboration with the Data Science Lab(DSL), specifically with Peter Dängeli (UniBe) and Levyn Bürki (UniBe).

The DSL will be entrusted with the management and preservation of digitized materials via IIIFservers, the development of prototypes, as well as support for the transcription and encodingprocess. The DSL is responsible for developing the advanced technical components, including theconfiguration of the workflow, the implementation of the TEI data model, and the automatedtransformation of OCR outputs into TEI format. The lab will also oversee the completion of thetechnical infrastructure in preparation for the official release of the digital edition.

In addition, the DSL plays an active role in designing the web application, developing bothinternal and external (front-end) interfaces, managing data distribution, and preparing for thepublic launch.
asl_text: The Swiss Literary Archives (SLA) of the Swiss National Library are a key partner in the project.In 2003, the Swiss Confederation acquired theestate of Alice Ceresa, on which it hasheld the rights since 2024. For this reason, a substantial part of the project’s corpus has beenselected from the SLA’s archival holdings, and the SLA also supports the research activities. TheNational Library furthermore provides the reproductions to be included in the digital edition.
ceresa_text:
Alice Ceresa (Basel, 25 January 1923 - Rome, 22 December 2001) spends her childhood between Baseland Bellinzona. At her father’s insistence, she pursues commercial studies, but starts to writeout of personal passion, contributing to the daily newspaper «Il Dovere». In the early 1940s shemoves to Zurich, where she collaborates with magazines and newspapers such as «Die Weltwoche» and«Svizzera italiana». During this period, she meets Franco Fortini and Ignazio Silone, andbefriends the writer, pianist, and Jungian psychoanalyst Aline Valangin, who in those difficultyears is hosting many exiled intellectuals at her home in Comologno, Canton Ticino.

After periods in France and in French-speaking Switzerland, and through Silone’s mediation, Ceresasettles in Rome in 1950. There, she publishes in magazines such as «Botteghe Oscure», and laterbecomes involved with the Italian avant-garde, while always preserving a strong stylistic andconceptual independence. She is also active as a translator from German, often working with thepublisher Longanesi and introducing Italian readers to works by authors such as Elias Canetti andGerold Späth.

Ceresa makes her fiction debut in 1943 with the short story Gli altri, published in«Svizzera italiana». She rises to prominence in 1967 with the experimental novelLa figlia prodiga(Einaudi), the first title in the “La ricerca letteraria” series and winner of the Viareggio Prizefor Opera prima that same year. In 1979 she publishes the short story La morte del padrein «Nuovi Argomenti», and in 1990 the novel Bambine (Einaudi), which wins the Schiller Prize and offersan acute and pungent portrayal of childhood and patriarchal structures. From the 1970s onwards, sheworks on the Piccolo dizionario dell’inuguaglianza femminile, published posthumously(nottetempo 2007, 2020).

Her work, centred on the female condition and on the search for linguistic forms capable ofrepresenting it, combines formal rigour, irony, and ethical tension. Today, Alice Ceresa isrecognised as a significant voice in twentieth-century feminist and experimental writing.Translated into French and German during her lifetime, her major works have recently beenreissued, and her writing is met with renewed critical interest.
